sentences.text: I must say at this point that when we crossed over the German border into Czechoslovakia-- as you know, [INAUDIBLE] started in Czechoslovakia-- the population, some of them risked their lives to throw food onto our train.
We passed villages.
I remember one village we stopped.
And it was only a little place.
And they started bringing out whatever they had.
They didn't have any- they themselves didn't have much to eat.
And, they brought out- And they didn't have any more bread, so they brought out food and whatever they could, they threw at us.
And we lapped it up, you know?
That was over the Czechs.
And I found them very nice people.
And I shall never forget that.
To this day, I went back to Prague two years ago, and I especially went back to Prague for that reason.
I've never been back to Poland yet.
And for the Czech people I feel very grateful for what they've done for us at that time.
Because they suffered under the, um, they suffered a lot from the Germans.
You heard of [INAUDIBLE].
They razed a whole village in Czechoslovakia.
They- they- they killed all the people, and when they shot the [INAUDIBLE] I've forgotten his name, [INAUDIBLE] I think.
I can't remember.
The Czech partisans shot the German [INAUDIBLE] of Czechoslovakia.
And so in reprisal, they shot a whole village, women and children.
They razed it to the ground, not a single house they left in that place.
The Czech really hated the Germans.
And also they suffered a lot because a lot of- when Germany moved into Sudentenland they deported a lot of Czechs back into-- and I can't remember the amount.
So the Czechs suffered a lot from the Germans.
That was one of the reasons.
When we stopped outside Theresienstadt-- so, the German guards were also very exhausted.
They had been with us for four weeks without any change of clothes or any rest.
So the Czech partisans came along-- we didn't know they were partisans-- but they came along, and they took this picture.
All right.
They took-- I got hold of this picture afterwards, not at the time when they took it.
And they took this picture of us, and they asked the guards if they would like a bath and some food, some hot food.
So naturally they jumped to it.
So they took them off from the train, and they went away with them.
And we never saw these guards again.
They never came back.
And we were taken in-- but this was still- the Germans were still in control of this area.
So we didn't know what's happening.
We were taken into the ghetto, the Theresienstadt ghetto.
And we were taken into a bath, and as we came into the bath, we undressed.
And in the middle of-- I keep saying bath.
It was showers.
In the middle of having this shower, there was a direct hit, and the lights went out, a hit from a- not from a bomb, from a heavy artillery shell.
And we all fell to the floor.
And we lay on the floor like this.
And there was a lot of shelling going on from both sides.
On one side the Russians were shelling, and the other side the Germans.
And we were in a sort of a valley.
And it carried on for a few hours.
And then in the morning, it was still night one of the boys got up, looked out of the window, and he heard a noise.
We heard something's going on outside, shouting.
And he looked and saw soldiers running around with machine guns.
But they didn't know whether they were German soldiers or Russian soldiers.
There was a lot of shooting going on.
After about a half hour it died down, the shooting.
And somebody shouted, the Russians are here.
And we all got up, and it was all over.
We went out into the street, and we saw the Russian tanks coming.
And we jumped onto the tanks, and we drove in-- it was already getting daylight in the morning.
And we drove into a little town there called [INAUDIBLE], which was not very far from Theresienstadt.
And we came into this town on the Russian tanks.
I must say the Russians were very good to us at the time.
And they said, you can do what you like.
And you went into the- there were a lot of German shops there.
So we started raiding these German shops.
And I got hold of-- we children-- I was a youngster.
But I didn't have the adults looked for jewelry, valuables, what to get hold of.
We were only interested in one thing, food, and food, and food only.
So, we- I got-- we- we managed to get, uh, break into a shop that was selling all kinds of tinned in jars conserves, apples, pears, blackberries.
So I got hold of a box of blackberry jars.
And I run out into the street with this box of blackberry jars.
I got hold of a German girl passed with a bicycle.
So I told her to get off the bicycle.
And I put this box on the back of the bicycle.
And that's how I drove back into the ghetto with the jars of blackberries.
And we all started eating these blackberries.
And the other boys came back with bread, with meat.
Some- one boy came back with the head of a pig.
Somebody they killed a cow.
They tore it into pieces.
They brought back meat.
And they started cooking the meat.
You can imagine what it was like.
Anyway after a few days, the [INAUDIBLE]
Well I felt delighted.
It was all over.
I couldn't believe it.
It felt on top of the world.
I didn't have time to think what happened to my parents and to the rest of my family.
I was glad I'm alive.
And I have my brother with me.
And I couldn't believe it was all over.
It was like a dream.
It's very hard to describe.
And then the [INAUDIBLE] started bringing parcels.
And they started distributing tins of meat and fatty foods, which our stomachs could not take anymore.
So we were allotted a room-- when I say we, I kept together with a bunch of boys, all youngsters-- and we were given a room about 20 or 25 in that room.
And we all slept on the floor.
There were no beds or furniture in that room.
But to us it was like paradise.
And we started eating the parcels from the [INAUDIBLE].
And after a few days one of the boys didn't feel well.
So a Russian doctor came, and he examined him.
And he said he thinks he's got typhoid.
So they took him away.
The next day, two or three went down the same symptoms.
Finally we all went down with the typhoid.
And I was also taken away.
And we were- they didn't have hospitals.
So what they had done was they took furniture and [INAUDIBLE], and they put them-- they turned them over flat on the floor.
And they put some straw on it.
And that was our hospital.
They had no medicines.
So the only medicines was vodka.
I remember I was laying there.
It was with typhoid, you have a very high fever, and one doesn't know what's going on.
But like in my dream I felt somebody was pouring down something in my mouth.
It was vodka.
I later found out that's all they had.
They themselves the Russians had nothing.
They had no medicines.
And it's-- you can't compare the Russian army or the-- to the British or the German army.
The Russians when they came, when I saw them first, their rifles had a bit of string.
It didn't have a belt even.
Everything was improvised.
They suffered a lot.
The Russians lost 20 million people.
And when they finished up with us, they had nothing.
Anyway, after being a few weeks in a sort of what you call it a hospital, gradually, we- a lot of boys, a lot of men, boys died from the typhoid.
But me and my brother we managed to get over it.
But we were still very weak.
And every country sent trains to take away their nationals home to repatriate.
So the Polish government also sent the train to take their nationals.
But as soon as we heard that the Polish government sent a train, and they were coming to take us away back to Poland, we run away from the hospital, discharged ourselves.
We didn't want to go back to Poland.
Poland to us was one big graveyard.
We knew there was nothing there to go back to.
And to this day, I have never gone back to Poland.
And after I managed to recover a bit, we were given permission by the Russian commandant that was in charge of the ghetto to bring in German prisoners to do- to clean up the place.
We gave them back a bit of the medicine that they gave us.
And it's amazing.
We were skeletons.
And, we- and I could go up to a higher ranking officer.
He was 6 foot tall.
And if he would have given one blow with his mouth, I would have fallen down.
I wasonly- I was very weak.
And he was terrified of me.
And whatever I asked him to do, he did.
It felt very good.
I must say, it felt very good.
But some people they had it in them the first three days, the Russians allowed to do what we like with the Germans.
But I didn't- I-I didn't first of all, I didn't have the strength-- it's not so easy to kill somebody.
Some people, there was a river there, the Vltava, and the river was mined.
The Germans mined it.
Because they didn't want the Russians to cross over.
And some people got hold of, especially SS, if they knew they SS men, they got hold of them, and they threw them into the river, and that was [INAUDIBLE] certain death.
Because as soon as they fell down, they exploded.
The mines killed them.
But what we did do with the Germans, we brought them into work.
And in the evening, we took them back to the-- there was a POW-- a prisoner of war camp.
Yes, it's got to be now.
All right.
After we recovered-- [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] We-- we tried to do what we can to-- we picked out the SS.
You may how we knew they were SS.
They had tattooed under the, uh- here, so we picked out the SS.
Because I knew the SS were the ones that did most of the atrocities.
And we beat them up as much as we could.
We made them work.
And we made fun of them like they made fun of us.
But after a week or two, the Russians clamped down on that.
They didn't allow any more to make, um, to do anything to the Germans.
And we were in Theresienstadt for about two months.
There were all different--
Well at the time yes, at the time I must say, it was vengeance, yes.
I mean we were only human.
And now I suppose I feel different, because not all Germans are bad.
And I don't- I don't suppose not all Germans were bad then.
But after what we went through, we- we did the first few days with SS-- I never killed anybody in my life.
But, uh- it- I- first of all, I didn't have the strength to kill.
And I don't suppose it's so easy to kill a person.
But some people did kill them.
Some Russians killed them as well.
They used to take them into the forest not far from there, and they shot them in that forest.
They picked out SS, and they killed some of them.
The first few days, but then it stopped.
Then they didn't allow it anymore.
And we were in Theresienstadt until-- there were different rumors going around that we going to go to Switzerland, we we going to go to Palestine.
One thing we didn't want to go back was Poland.
We didn't want to go back to Poland.
So then we heard that we going to go to England, that the British government is allowing thousand boys and some girls as well.
There weren't many girls left alive.
So we were told to start learning a few words in English, the basic [INAUDIBLE].
And after sometime I think it was-- we were liberated in May.
This must have been in July.
We were taken to Prague.
And in Prague, we were about two weeks in Prague, because the weather was no- was bad, and the planes couldn't come over.
They sent over bombers, not passenger planes, for us.
So they couldn't takeoff.
They couldn't land.
We were two weeks in Prague.
And that two weeks I must say the Czech people wherever we went, they could see we were ex-people from the concentration camps, the way we were dressed and the way we looked.
If we went into a restaurant to get some food, they wouldn't take any money from us.
If we went on buses or trams, they wouldn't take the fare money from us.
And we used to- there were a lot of German prisoners of war that did work in the streets.
They cleaned the streets.
And we used to go up to say to the Czechs, we will show them how to work, to the Germans, you know.
You make the Germans work.
We will show them how to work.
And they used to beat them, the Germans.
And the Czechs, they clapped us on the backs, good.
This went on for about two weeks.
So we got onto the planes, and we came over to England.
And we landed in a place, in Carlisle, I think was, nearby the airport.
And from there we were put on coaches and taken to the lake district, [INAUDIBLE].
And we were put into little huts.
It was a-- the war-- it was-- there was an aircraft factory, and the workers lived in these little huts.
And it had a-- it contained a bunk and a little wardrobe and a small table with a chair.
But to us, this was paradise.
And then we started getting food.
We came-- they had a big dining hall where we all went into to eat.
I must say the British government gave permission to bring over thousand boys, and-- but they couldn't find thousand boys.
They only managed to get together 750 or 740.
They searched over all of Germany and Czechoslovakia, and they couldn't find thousand boys.
Because the boys my age and younger were all killed off.
So anyway, we-- we-- we started eating.
They bring in bread and butter and porridge in the morning.
We got porridge.
And the bread kept disappearing from the tables.
And the people that were feeding us, they couldn't understand.
They're bringing in hundreds and hundreds of loaves of bread, and it's all disappearing.
Because we were so we used to hide food, we couldn't believe that there would ever be a time when we would have enough food.
So we used to hide the food under the table, and we used to carry it into our rooms and hide it in the rooms.
That went on for about a week or two weeks until finally the bread was moldy, and we realized there's no need for it.
We would get plenty of it.
So we stopped doing that.
And gradually, we started putting on weight.
We put on too much weight, because all we ate was bread and porridge.
So we got very fat.
And we were there for three months until after three months, we were distributed all over the country into hostels.
And I ended up in London with another 15 boys in a hostel.
And I was in this hostel for about two years.
I went to school.
I went to college.
And then I started-- I learned-- started learning a trade.
And that's it.
That's my story.
After- it took-it took some time.
It took some time after we came over to England, and we started-- you see, even in Theresienstadt, we were together.
It wasn't a normal life.
We were together.
It was people that had suffered a lot.
Coming over here we got- we came in touch with normal people, and we had lessons.
We were told how to behave.
And we realized there's no need to tell lies.
You see all the time, you could only survive if you told lies.
And if they told you to go here, you went there.
Because you knew if you- they tell you to go there, that's bad for you.
You had to scheme.
Although however young we were, we were grown up.
We were children, but we were grown up in our minds.
We were grown up, because we had to scheme how to survive all the time.
I mean, you couldn't exist under normal circumstances.
You had to try to escape from-- from-- I-- I-- I'll give you an example.
I remember a case that happened in Auschwitz.
A Hungarian Jew was there.
He was a doctor.
They came straight from Hungary.
He didn't really- he was only two months in Auschwitz when they bought him from Hungary.
And SS men started beating him.
We were working- moving some stones, any work to make us tired.
There was no point in this type of work.
So we had to move stones from place to the other.
And this doctor, he also started moving the stones.
So this SS man started beating him.
So this doctor turned around and said to him, do you know who I am?
You're beating me.
Do you realize who I am?
I was one of the greatest professors in Budapest.
And here you're beating me.
He thought he will reason with this man.
And he will treat him like a person.
But to these SS men, they were all like animals, worse than animals.
Animals they gave food.
I wish they would have looked after us like they looked after their Alsatian dogs.
So he-- he beat him.
He beat him up after that that this man was bleeding like [INAUDIBLE].
The mentality of these people was different to us.
We were already acclimatized.
When I came to Auschwitz, I was already acclimatized to it.
I had 4 and 1/2 years of good schooling, you understand.
Nothing shocked me anymore.
I mean, they took away my mother and my sister.
I-- I-- I wasn't shocked anymore.
I didn't cry any more.
I didn't have any tears anymore left.
Most of us were like zombies.
One- one can't- it took years here to get that out of my-- even now I sometimes I get, especially when I speak, it comes back.
I get nightmares at night.
It's impossible to describe what one feels after so many years.
I remember the first time in Prague, that was, I was crossing-- I was frightened to cross a road.
We weren't not used to how to cross a road.
It took weeks till I got used to the traffic.
It's a different-- I suppose it's the same today.
You can't compare.
But if a person comes out from prison after he's been in for 10, 15 years, it also takes time to adjust to.
Which is a prison today, to us it would have been like paradise.
You can't compare a death camp to a prison.
But I think most of the people, most of the boys that I am in contact with, we came out fairly normal.
I don't hate anybody anymore after 50 years.
It's a new generation anyway.
So let's hope these things will never happen again.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That's all I have to say.
I-- later on in 1953, I met my dear wife, and we got married.
And it was the year after coronation.
And when we got married I remember the streets were all lined with flags.
And to us, the flags were out for us.
That's how we felt.
And we got married, and thank god we got children and grandchildren.
And I think I've made a good job of it after all that.
